  Fit with the job, organization, team, and supervisor predicts satisfaction, commitment, performance, and turnover intentions. Interesting tasks matter, but culture, manager, and team fit also matter.

  People often overestimate the effect of income on experienced well-being because income is salient when making comparisons. This is relevant when a higher offer may draw attention away from the lived experience of the work.

Pay level has only a weak-to-moderate relationship with job satisfaction. Higher salary matters, but it is usually not among the strongest predictors of being satisfied at work.

**“The Value of Bosses” — Lazear, Shaw & Stanton (2015).**  
Boss quality has large effects on worker productivity and development. A move into leading a small team could matter substantially, but its value depends on whether management itself is motivating and whether the environment supports good leadership.

answer:
**Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature** — Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007).  
Autonomy, task significance, skill variety, feedback, and other “interesting work” features strongly predict job satisfaction, intrinsic motivation, and lower turnover intentions. This supports taking work content very seriously, not treating salary/title as the main variables.

**The Validity of the Job Characteristics Model: A Review and Meta-Analysis** — Fried & Ferris (1987).  
Core job characteristics such as autonomy, identity, significance, variety, and feedback are reliably associated with motivation and satisfaction. The evidence supports “interesting, well-designed work matters a lot,” though performance effects are more mixed.

**Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis** — Cerasoli, Nicklin, & Ford (2014).  
Intrinsic motivation predicts performance especially for complex, creative, quality-focused work. Extrinsic incentives also matter, so the evidence argues against focusing on interestingness “almost exclusively.”

**A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation** — Deci, Koestner, & Ryan (1999).  
Expected tangible rewards can reduce intrinsic motivation for already-interesting tasks, especially when they feel controlling. This suggests higher pay or status can be less valuable if the role becomes less intrinsically engaging.

**The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature** — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010).  
Pay level is positively related to job and pay satisfaction, but the relationship is modest. This supports the idea that once pay is good enough and fair, work content and fit often dominate.

**Inequality at Work: The Effect of Peer Salaries on Job Satisfaction** — Card, Mas, Moretti, & Saez (2012).  
Workers who learned they were paid below peers reported lower job satisfaction and higher job-search intentions, while above-median pay produced little corresponding satisfaction boost. Salary matters especially through fairness and relative standing, not just absolute dollars.

**Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit** — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005).  
Person–job and person–organization fit are strongly associated with satisfaction, commitment, performance, and lower turnover. If one role is much better aligned with your interests and values, that fit is empirically important.

**Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work** — Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin, & Schwartz (1997).  
People who experience their work as a calling tend to report higher work and life satisfaction. This supports prioritizing meaningful or intrinsically compelling work, especially in careers where identity and purpose are central.

**The Call of the Wild: Zookeepers, Callings, and the Double-Edged Sword of Deeply Meaningful Work** — Bunderson & Thompson (2009).  
Deeply meaningful work can create strong satisfaction and identity, but also willingness to accept sacrifices, undercompensation, and exploitation. This cautions against treating “interesting work” as the only thing that matters.

**The Value of Bosses** — Lazear, Shaw, & Stanton (2015).  
Good bosses substantially improve worker productivity, showing that management can be a high-leverage and valuable role. If leading a team is intrinsically motivating to you, the leadership component may itself be meaningful work rather than merely a title upgrade.

**Promotions and the Peter Principle** — Benson, Li, & Shue (2019).  
High individual contributors are often promoted into management, but the skills that predict IC performance do not necessarily predict managerial performance. This suggests evaluating “leading a small team” as a distinct job with distinct sources of satisfaction, not simply as career progression.

**Does Living in California Make People Happy? A Focusing Illusion in Judgments of Life Satisfaction** — Schkade & Kahneman (1998).  
People often overestimate the effect of salient differences when imagining future happiness. In career choices, salary, title, prestige, and even “interestingness” can be overweighted unless you simulate the daily work realistically.

- **“Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory” — Hackman & Oldham, 1976.** Jobs high in autonomy, skill variety, task significance, task identity, and feedback predict stronger internal motivation, satisfaction, and performance. This supports giving substantial weight to intrinsically engaging work, especially if the current role offers autonomy and mastery.

- **“Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit” — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005.** Person–job and person–organization fit are strongly associated with job satisfaction, commitment, and lower turnover. - **“Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of Over 60 Years of Research” — Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow, 2012.** Interest fit predicts job performance, training performance, and persistence across many studies. This supports the idea that “interesting work” is not just a luxury but can affect long-term career output.

- **“Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis” — Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford, 2014.** Intrinsic motivation predicts both quality and quantity of performance, especially for complex work. Extrinsic incentives also matter, so the evidence favors high weight on interest but not an “almost exclusive” focus.

- **“A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation” — Deci, Koestner & Ryan, 1999.** Tangible rewards can reduce intrinsic motivation when they feel controlling, especially for already-interesting tasks. Higher salary is not necessarily harmful, but it is unlikely to replace lost intrinsic engagement in complex research work.

- **“The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature” — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010.** Pay level has only a modest relationship with job satisfaction. This suggests a somewhat higher salary should usually be discounted relative to day-to-day work quality, unless the money changes your life constraints meaningfully.

- **“Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved” — Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers, 2023.** Emotional well-being generally rises with income, but the marginal effects vary and are not the whole story. This weakens any claim that salary is irrelevant, while still implying that pay should be weighed against work content and fit.

- **“Affect and Creativity at Work” — Amabile, Barsade, Mueller & Staw, 2005.** Positive affect at work predicts creativity, including effects that carry into subsequent days. - **“The Significance of Task Significance: Job Performance Effects, Relational Mechanisms, and Boundary Conditions” — Grant, 2008.** Seeing the meaningful impact of one’s work can increase persistence and performance. - **“Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work” — Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz, 1997.** People who experience their work as a calling tend to report higher satisfaction and meaning. - **“Toward a Theory of Individual Differences and Leadership: Understanding the Motivation to Lead” — Chan & Drasgow, 2001.** Motivation to lead is a distinct individual difference that predicts leadership development and leadership-role interest.
